__inputs.name: DS_PROMETHEUS
__inputs.label: Prometheus
__inputs.description: The Prometheus datasource scraping Home Assistant's own /api/prometheus endpoint (the built-in `prometheus:` integration), not meater-golang directly.

title: MEATER Monitor (via Home Assistant)
description:
Reads the meater_golang entities back out of Home Assistant's own Prometheus exporter (see docs/home-assistant.md for the entity list, and HA's `prometheus:` integration for how to enable /api/prometheus). See meater-native.json for the variant that scrapes meater-golang's own /metrics endpoint directly instead.

Home Assistant emits several metrics per entity that all share the same `entity` label: the actual value (named per-domain, e.g. `homeassistant_sensor_temperature_celsius`), plus generic cross-domain metadata like `homeassistant_entity_available` and a last-updated/last-changed timestamp. A bare `{entity="..."}` selector matches all of them at once, which shows up as extra duplicate series full of huge Unix-timestamp numbers or stray 0/1s. To avoid that, every query here also filters `__name__` down to the domain's metric prefix (`homeassistant_sensor_.*` or `homeassistant_binary_sensor_.*`), which is specific to the real value metric and excludes HA's generic per-entity metadata metrics. If a panel is still empty or looks wrong, open your HA instance's /api/prometheus output and search for the entity_id to see exactly what metric name(s) it produced, then adjust the query's __name__ filter.

list.name: entity_prefix
list.label: HA device slug
list.description: The entity_id prefix Home Assistant gave the MEATER Monitor device, e.g. sensor.<this>_internal_temperature. Defaults to the integration's default device name, slugified.

panels.title: Temperatures
panels.description: Tip (meat), ambient (chamber), and target, in whatever unit system your HA instance uses.

panels.title: Tip temperature
panels.description: Plain current tip (meat) reading, on a fixed 0-100 range with generic raw/cooking/well-done reference bands (adjust if your HA instance reports °F; not tied to this cook's actual target — see the Tip vs target gauge for that).

panels.title: Tip vs target
panels.description: Tip (meat) temperature as a percentage of this cook's target, so the color is meaningful no matter what target was set: red while still well below it, yellow as it closes in, green at/near target. See the Temperatures panel for the exact reading.

panels.title: Ambient temperature
panels.description: Current chamber/ambient temperature. There's no target sensor for ambient, so this uses a fixed 0-300 range (adjust if your HA instance reports °F) rather than being colored relative to a target.

panels.title: Cook progress
panels.description: Home Assistant's cook_progress sensor is already 0-100 (a percent), unlike the native dashboard's 0-1 ratio.

panels.title: Time to target
panels.description: In seconds. Home Assistant clears this sensor to unavailable rather than publishing a negative sentinel, so a blank panel means unknown (e.g. a deep stall), same as the native dashboard's NaN.

panels.title: Rise rate
panels.description: °C/min (or °F/min, matching HA's unit system). This is a non-standard unit for HA's Prometheus exporter, so if this is empty, check /api/prometheus for the actual metric name.

panels.title: Ready
panels.description: Automate on this one — it's a plain binary_sensor, so it exports reliably as 0/1 regardless of HA version.

options.content: Cook state, estimate source, cook name, and meat type are text/enum sensors, and `ready_at`/`cook_started` are timestamps. Home Assistant's Prometheus exporter generally does not publish non-numeric sensor states as a scrapeable value, so they're left off this dashboard — view them directly in Home Assistant, or on [meater-native.json](meater-native.json) if you scrape meater-golang directly instead.
